ground_truth: Articles 26(4) and 26(5) establish the deployer's obligations when problems with High-Risk AI are detected: (1) Article 26(4) — monitoring and suspension obligation: deployers must monitor the operation of the High-Risk AI system on the basis of instructions for use; if the deployer has reason to believe that the use of the High-Risk AI in accordance with its instructions is not compliant with the AI Act (including due to poor outputs for a specific customer segment suggesting bias or inaccuracy), the deployer must: inform the provider or distributor without undue delay; suspend use of the system where relevant; (2) Article 26(5) — serious risk notification: if the deployer identifies a serious risk to health, safety, or fundamental rights, they must immediately inform the provider of the AI system AND the relevant market surveillance authority in the Member State where the deployer is established; (3) What 'reason to believe' non-compliance means for the credit AI scenario: unexpectedly poor outputs for a specific customer segment could indicate: data representation failure (the model's training data did not adequately represent this segment); systematic bias that was not detected before deployment; model drift affecting certain segments disproportionately; (4) Suspension threshold — Article 26(4) says 'where relevant'; the bank must assess whether the issue poses sufficient risk to justify suspension; for a fundamental rights issue like discriminatory credit outcomes, suspension of AI-driven decisions for the affected segment pending investigation is appropriate; (5) Communication to affected customers — while not explicitly required by Art.26, the fundamental rights implications of systematic bias in credit decisions warrant proactive customer communication; (6) The AI Review Committee should ensure this monitoring and notification process is documented in advance.
notes: Art.26 모니터링 및 사용 중단; 배포자의 사전·사후 의무

question: Article 99 of the EU AI Act establishes penalties for non-compliance. What are the penalty levels for financial institutions that violate High-Risk AI obligations, and how should the AI Review Committee factor these into governance prioritization?
ground_truth: Article 99 establishes a tiered penalty structure for EU AI Act violations: (1) Tier 1 — prohibited practices (Art.5): up to €35 million or 7% of total worldwide annual turnover (whichever is higher); for a large Korean bank, 7% of global turnover could be billions; these are the most severe penalties and apply to absolute prohibitions; (2) Tier 2 — High-Risk AI non-compliance (Chapter III obligations, Chapter V GPAI obligations, Art.13 transparency, Art.26 deployer obligations): up to €15 million or 3% of total worldwide annual turnover; this tier covers the bulk of financial AI governance obligations; (3) Tier 3 — provision of incorrect information (Art.86, Art.91): up to €7.5 million or 1% of total worldwide annual turnover; (4) SME and startup moderation — for SMEs and startups, national competent authorities may impose reduced penalties proportionate to size; (5) Governance prioritization implications for the AI Review Committee: the penalty calculation based on worldwide turnover means large global banks face potentially massive penalties for seemingly local EU AI Act violations; prioritize zero-tolerance compliance for: Article 5 prohibited practices (Tier 1 — existential penalty risk); Article 9 risk management, Article 14 human oversight, Article 26 deployer obligations (Tier 2 — material penalty risk); high-risk AI registration and technical documentation; (6) Penalty assessment factors — national authorities consider: nature, gravity, and duration; whether harm was intentional or negligent; mitigating actions; financial capacity; (7) The penalty structure makes EU AI Act compliance economically rational even for institutions that treat compliance as a cost-benefit calculation.
notes: Art.99 제재; 전 세계 매출 기반 과징금

question: How should the AI Review Committee plan for future changes to the EU AI Act, including potential expansions of the High-Risk AI list under Article 7?
ground_truth: The EU AI Act is designed to evolve with AI technology. Key mechanisms the AI Review Committee must monitor: (1) Article 7 — Amendments to Annex III: the European Commission may amend Annex III through delegated acts to add or modify High-Risk AI categories when AI systems pose sufficient risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights; financial AI categories that could be added include: investment advisory AI; comprehensive customer financial scoring AI; AI used in systemic risk management; (2) Article 112 — Evaluation and Review: by August 2030 (5 years after full application), the Commission must evaluate and report to Parliament on the functioning of the AI Act, including whether to amend the High-Risk AI classification criteria; this establishes a formal regulatory evolution cycle; (3) Delegated and implementing acts — numerous provisions authorize Commission delegated acts and implementing acts that will fill in technical details; monitor Official Journal publications; (4) Technical standards — harmonized technical standards developed by CEN/CENELEC and other standardization bodies will operationalize many vague AI Act requirements; monitor standards development and adopt standards as they are published; (5) AI Office guidelines (Art.96) — the Commission will issue guidelines on AI Act implementation; for financial AI, these guidelines may address specific questions about credit scoring, insurance pricing, and investment AI; (6) AI Review Committee forward planning — conduct an annual regulatory horizon scan; maintain a tracker of delegated acts, implementing acts, and Annex III amendment proposals; build governance processes that can accommodate regulatory evolution without requiring fundamental restructuring.
notes: EU AI Act 진화; Annex III 확장 모니터링

question: Article 6(3) of the EU AI Act allows providers to argue that an Annex III AI system is not actually High-Risk because it does not pose significant risk. Can the bank argue that its credit scoring AI is not High-Risk despite being listed in Annex III §5(b)?
ground_truth: Article 6(3) creates an exception to Annex III High-Risk classification: an AI system that is listed in Annex III shall NOT be considered High-Risk if it does not pose a significant risk of harm to health, safety, or the fundamental rights of natural persons. However, this exception has narrow applicability: (1) Exception criteria (Art.6(3)) — the AI system is not High-Risk if: (a) it performs a narrow procedural task; (b) it improves the result of a previously completed human activity; (c) it detects decision-making patterns or deviations from prior decision-making patterns and is not meant to replace or influence the previously completed human assessment without proper human review; or (d) it performs a preparatory task to an assessment relevant for the purpose listed in Annex III; (2) Application to credit scoring AI — a credit scoring AI that makes or directly influences credit approval/rejection decisions does NOT qualify for this exception; it is not merely performing a narrow procedural task or preparing for human assessment; it is making substantive judgments that directly affect credit access; (3) Narrow exception scope — the exception is designed for AI systems that assist in administrative tasks related to Annex III activities, not for AI that is the primary decision mechanism; (4) Unjustified non-High-Risk claims — Article 80 addresses situations where providers unjustifiably classify Annex III AI as non-High-Risk; market surveillance authorities can investigate and require High-Risk compliance; (5) Documentation requirement — if the bank's credit AI were to invoke Art.6(3), it must document the reasoning and notify the market surveillance authority per Art.49(2); the AI Review Committee should not invoke Art.6(3) for credit scoring AI.
notes: Art.6(3) 비고위험 예외; 신용 AI에 적용 불가 이유

question: The bank's AML AI system screens transactions and customer profiles for money laundering indicators. The AI Review Committee must determine whether this qualifies as High-Risk AI under the EU AI Act's Annex III.
ground_truth: AML AI classification requires careful analysis of Annex III categories: (1) Annex III §6 — Law Enforcement: §6(a) covers AI assessing the risk of a natural person becoming a victim of criminal offences; §6(d) covers AI for assessing risk of a person offending or re-offending; §6(e) covers AI for profiling natural persons during criminal investigation; these provisions are explicitly limited to use 'by or on behalf of law enforcement authorities' or 'Union institutions in support of law enforcement'; (2) Private bank AML AI — a bank's internal AML monitoring AI is not operated 'by or on behalf of law enforcement authorities'; it operates for regulatory compliance (Anti-Money Laundering Directive compliance) and internal fraud prevention; it is not a law enforcement tool; therefore, Annex III §6 does not classify private bank AML AI as High-Risk; (3) Other Annex III categories — AML AI does not clearly fit other Annex III categories (it is not credit scoring, insurance pricing, employment AI, etc.); (4) Current assessment — private bank AML transaction monitoring AI is NOT High-Risk under Annex III as currently written; (5) Article 50 transparency — AML AI does not typically interact directly with customers in a way triggering Art.50; however, if the AI generates customer-facing communications about account restrictions, disclosure obligations may apply; (6) Independent financial regulatory obligations — AML AI compliance is primarily governed by AMLD6/Anti-Money Laundering Regulation rather than EU AI Act; (7) AI Review Committee approach — apply robust governance despite non-High-Risk classification given the significant impact on customer account access rights.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Annex III; Article 6
notes: AML AI 분류; 법집행 목적 AI와 구분

subcategory: EU AI Act and Environmental Considerations
question: The bank's ESG team asks whether the EU AI Act includes any provisions addressing the environmental impact of AI systems. How should the AI Review Committee respond?
ground_truth: The EU AI Act does not include explicit mandatory provisions on AI environmental impact or energy consumption comparable to its human rights and safety requirements. However: (1) Recital considerations — some EU AI Act recitals reference sustainability and environmental considerations as context for the regulation; these do not create binding obligations but signal regulatory direction; (2) Potential connection to Article 9 risk management — the Article 9(1) risk management system broadly covers 'risks to health, safety or fundamental rights'; environmental harm from AI (e.g., massive energy consumption contributing to climate risk) could theoretically be included in a comprehensive risk management framework, though this is not explicitly required; (3) Technical documentation (Art.11, Annex IV) — the technical documentation requirements do not explicitly require energy consumption disclosure, unlike some proposed AI reporting frameworks; (4) EU regulatory evolution — the European Commission has committed to monitoring AI's environmental impact; future amendments to the AI Act or complementary regulations (AI Liability Directive, ESG reporting frameworks) may introduce environmental AI obligations; (5) CSRD connection — banks subject to the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) must report on climate-related risks and impacts; AI energy consumption may need to be included in this reporting even if not required by the AI Act directly; (6) AI Review Committee practical response — while not an EU AI Act requirement, including AI environmental impact assessment in governance as a voluntary best practice: satisfies CSRD reporting needs; demonstrates comprehensive ESG commitment; aligns with NIST AI RMF MEASURE 2.12 environmental impact assessment requirement.
notes: EU AI Act 환경 조항 부재; CSRD와 연계

question: The AI Review Committee must understand the EU AI Act's timeline for applicability. What is the phased implementation schedule under Article 113, and what obligations are already in force for financial institutions?
ground_truth: Article 113 of the EU AI Act establishes a phased implementation timeline: (1) Entry into force — the Regulation entered into force on 1 August 2024 (20 days after publication in the Official Journal on 12 July 2024); (2) Phase 1 — 6 months after entry into force (February 2025): Chapter I (general provisions) and Chapter II (prohibited AI practices, Art.5) become applicable; financial institutions must immediately comply with Article 5 prohibited AI practice prohibitions as of February 2025; (3) Phase 2 — 12 months (August 2025): Chapter V (General-Purpose AI Models) and Chapter VII (Governance) become applicable; GPAI model obligations and EU governance structure are operational; (4) Phase 3 — 24 months (August 2026): the majority of the Regulation becomes applicable, including: Chapter III (High-Risk AI requirements — Art.9-15, 16-27); Chapter IV (Transparency obligations — Art.50); Chapter VIII (EU database); Chapter IX (Post-market monitoring, market surveillance); this is the critical phase for financial AI governance; (5) Phase 4 — 36 months (August 2027): Annex I (harmonized Union legislation) High-Risk AI becomes applicable; (6) For financial institutions (as of May 2026 — current date): Art.5 prohibited practices are already applicable (since February 2025); GPAI model obligations are applicable (since August 2025); High-Risk AI Chapter III obligations are applicable (since August 2026); (7) AI Review Committee priority — all High-Risk financial AI (credit scoring, insurance pricing) must be fully compliant with Chapter III requirements NOW (as of August 2026 application date).
notes: EU AI Act 단계적 시행; 현재 시점(2026.5) 기준 의무 적용 현황

question: Article 26(7) of the EU AI Act requires deployers of High-Risk AI to inform workers and their representatives about the use of High-Risk AI in the workplace. How does this apply to loan officers using the credit scoring AI?
ground_truth: Article 26(7) requires deployers to inform workers and worker representatives about the use of High-Risk AI systems in the workplace before such use begins. For loan officers using the credit scoring AI: (1) Scope — the obligation applies when the High-Risk AI is used to assist or replace human decision-making in work processes; using credit scoring AI to inform loan officer credit decisions is precisely this type of use; (2) What information must be provided — while the Act does not specify exact content, workers must be informed that High-Risk AI is being used and in what capacity; information should include: that the AI is classified as High-Risk under the EU AI Act; the purpose and capabilities of the credit scoring AI; how the AI output is intended to be used in the loan decision process; the human oversight role that loan officers are expected to perform; how loan officers can override or disregard AI recommendations; the monitoring and logging that occurs during AI use; (3) Timing — information must be provided before use begins; for new loan officers, this should be part of onboarding; for existing officers when the system is introduced, advance notice; (4) Workers' representatives — European Works Councils (EWCs) and national workers' councils must be informed for larger institutions; (5) Connection to Art.14 human oversight — worker information under Art.26(7) is foundational to the effective human oversight required by Art.14; loan officers cannot perform meaningful oversight of an AI system they were never informed about; (6) AI Review Committee obligation — ensure Art.26(7) worker information procedure is documented and implemented before deployment.
notes: Art.26(7) 근로자 정보 제공; 대출 담당자 AI 사용 고지

question: Article 15(3) of the EU AI Act requires technical robustness solutions including redundancy for High-Risk AI. How should the AI Review Committee implement cybersecurity and resilience measures for the credit scoring AI consistent with Article 15?
ground_truth: Article 15 requires High-Risk AI systems to achieve appropriate levels of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity throughout their lifecycle, maintained through state-of-the-art measures. Specific cybersecurity requirements for credit scoring AI: (1) Resilience against attempted manipulation (Art.15(1)) — the credit scoring AI must be resilient to attempts by third parties to alter its use, outputs, or performance through manipulation of the AI system, its components, or training data; key threats to address: adversarial input attacks — applicants crafting applications to game the AI scoring system; data poisoning — corrupting training data pipelines to bias future model outputs; prompt injection — for LLM-based components, injecting instructions that alter AI behavior; model extraction — reverse-engineering the scoring model through API access patterns; (2) Resilience against errors and faults (Art.15(2)) — the AI must be designed to be resilient to input errors, faults, or inconsistencies; fail-safe design: when inputs are anomalous or inconsistent (e.g., contradictory financial data), the system should flag for human review rather than producing a potentially erroneous score; (3) Redundancy solutions (Art.15(3)) — technical redundancy may be required appropriate to the risk; for credit scoring AI: hot standby scoring capability to maintain continuity if primary system fails; validated fallback scoring methodology (e.g., rule-based scorecard) for use during AI system unavailability; (4) DORA intersection — EU financial institutions are also subject to the Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA); DORA ICT risk management requirements and EU AI Act Art.15 cybersecurity requirements overlap substantially; the AI Review Committee should integrate compliance to avoid duplication.
notes: Art.15 사이버보안 요건; DORA와 연계

question: Article 9(2)(b) of the EU AI Act requires risk assessment under conditions of 'reasonably foreseeable misuse.' What foreseeable misuse scenarios must the AI Review Committee assess for the credit scoring AI?
ground_truth: Article 9(2)(b) requires estimation and evaluation of risks that may emerge when the High-Risk AI system is used under conditions of 'reasonably foreseeable misuse.' For credit scoring AI, reasonably foreseeable misuse scenarios include: (1) Gaming by applicants — applicants deliberately provide false information or manipulate the data they supply to improve their credit score; the AI should be robust to common gaming strategies (overstating income, disguising debt obligations); assess the model's vulnerability to such manipulation and implement validation checks; (2) Discriminatory use by deployer — the bank deploys the AI specifically targeting or excluding protected demographic groups, using the AI to rationalize discriminatory practices that the AI itself may not explicitly exhibit; the risk management system must include governance controls preventing discriminatory deployment even if the model itself is technically fair; (3) Use beyond intended purpose — using the credit scoring AI to assess creditworthiness for products it was not trained for (e.g., using a consumer credit AI for corporate lending); document the intended use boundaries and implement technical controls preventing out-of-scope use; (4) Overreliance without oversight — loan officers effectively outsourcing all credit judgment to the AI without meaningful oversight (automation bias); the risk management plan must address this specific form of misuse by designing oversight processes that maintain human judgment quality; (5) Secondary use of scoring data — using credit score outputs or model features for purposes beyond credit decisions (e.g., for marketing targeting, customer segmentation for other products) without appropriate consent; (6) Delayed update — continuing to use the AI after market conditions have changed materially, making the model's training data stale; (7) Document each misuse scenario and the corresponding mitigation in the Art.9 risk management system.
notes: 합리적 예측 가능한 오용 평가; 신용 AI 시나리오

question: Article 4 of the EU AI Act requires providers and deployers to take measures ensuring sufficient AI literacy of their staff. What AI literacy obligations does this create for the bank's AI Review Committee?
ground_truth: Article 4 of the EU AI Act requires providers and deployers of AI systems to take measures to ensure, to their best extent, a sufficient level of AI literacy of their staff and other persons dealing with the operation and use of AI systems on their behalf, taking into account their technical knowledge, experience, education, and training context, as well as considering the users the AI systems are intended to be used by. For the bank's AI Review Committee: (1) Scope of obligation — applies to all staff dealing with AI operation and use; for a bank this includes: AI developers and data scientists (deep technical literacy); AI Review Committee members (governance and risk literacy); loan officers and other business users of AI (operational literacy); compliance and legal staff (regulatory literacy); board members (strategic literacy); (2) Literacy content should be calibrated by role: technical staff — AI methods, model validation, bias detection; business users — AI capabilities and limitations, when to override, how to interpret outputs; governance staff — EU AI Act requirements, risk management frameworks, FRIA process; executives — AI governance principles, strategic risk implications; (3) Proportionality — 'to their best extent' and 'taking into account technical knowledge' allow proportionate implementation; a loan officer does not need to understand model architecture; (4) Connection to Art.14 human oversight — AI literacy is the foundation for effective human oversight; loan officers who lack literacy about the credit AI's capabilities and limitations cannot perform meaningful oversight; (5) Training program design — the AI Review Committee should develop a tiered AI literacy curriculum covering EU AI Act obligations, AI capabilities and limitations specific to each system, and governance procedures; (6) Record-keeping — maintain training completion records as evidence of Art.4 compliance.
notes: Art.4 AI 리터러시; 역할별 차등 교육

question: The bank's AI customer service chatbot is deployed in Germany to answer questions about banking products. Article 50(1) requires disclosure that the customer is interacting with an AI. What are the specific requirements and exceptions?
ground_truth: Article 50(1) requires providers of AI systems intended to interact directly with natural persons to design and develop the AI so that the natural persons are informed they are interacting with an AI system, in a timely, clear, and intelligible manner, and at the latest at the beginning of the first interaction. However, exceptions exist: (1) Evident from context exception — the disclosure obligation does not apply where it is evident from the context and the circumstances that the natural person is interacting with an AI system; if the bank's chatbot is clearly branded as 'AI Assistant' or accessed through a clearly labeled AI channel, the disclosure may be self-evident; (2) Law enforcement exception — disclosure may be waived where the AI system is authorized by law for detecting, preventing, investigating, or prosecuting crime; not applicable to banking chatbots; (3) Specific requirements: timing — disclosure must occur 'at the latest at the beginning of the first interaction' (not buried in terms and conditions or disclosed later); clarity — the disclosure must be clear and intelligible to the average user, not just technically present; (4) Practical implementation for banking chatbot: opening message should state explicitly 'You are now chatting with an AI assistant'; if the chat includes human handoff, notify when transitioning from AI to human and back; branding and interface design should reinforce the AI nature of the interaction; (5) Contrast with Article 50(2) — Art.50(1) covers interaction disclosure; Art.50(2) covers marking of AI-generated content; both may apply to the banking chatbot; (6) Enforcement — national competent authorities in Germany (BaFin or AI-specific authority) may investigate and penalize violations.
notes: Art.50(1) 챗봇 AI 고지; 첫 상호작용 시 의무

question: Article 49 of the EU AI Act requires registration of High-Risk AI systems in an EU database. What are the registration requirements for the bank's credit scoring AI, and who is responsible?
ground_truth: Article 49 requires registration of High-Risk AI systems listed in Annex III in the EU database established under Article 71. Registration requirements: (1) Provider registration obligation — before placing a High-Risk AI system on the EU market or putting it into service, the provider (not the deployer) must register the system in the EU database; for credit scoring AI, the provider (bank if internally developed; vendor if using external system) must register; (2) Registration content (Art.49(1)) — providers must submit information including: provider identity; High-Risk AI category under Annex III; AI system description, intended purpose, and main capabilities; system version information; EU Declaration of Conformity; contact information; countries where the system is or will be deployed; (3) Deployer registration for Annex III §2 (critical infrastructure) and §8 (justice/democracy) — deployers of these specific categories must also separately register their use; credit scoring AI under Annex III §5(b) does not appear to require additional deployer registration; (4) Database public access (Art.71) — the EU database is publicly accessible; registered AI systems are visible to the public; this creates transparency about what High-Risk AI is in use across the EU; (5) Update obligation — providers must update registration information when significant changes occur to the AI system; (6) For the bank as deployer (using vendor credit scoring AI): verify the vendor has registered the system before deploying in the EU; request registration confirmation and database entry identifier from the vendor; (7) AI Review Committee obligation — confirm EU database registration as a prerequisite for EU deployment approval.
notes: Art.49 EU 데이터베이스 등록; 공급자 의무

question: After implementing all feasible risk mitigations for the life insurance pricing AI, some residual risk of inaccurate pricing remains for rare medical conditions. Article 9(5) requires residual risk to be acceptable. How should the AI Review Committee determine acceptability?
ground_truth: Article 9(5) requires that risk management measures ensure: (a) the relevant residual risk associated with each hazard is judged to be acceptable; and (b) the overall residual risk of the High-Risk AI system is judged to be acceptable. The three-step hierarchy for residual risk acceptability: (1) Step 1 — Elimination or reduction of risks as far as technically feasible through design (Art.9(5)(a)) — for insurance pricing inaccuracy for rare conditions: attempt to expand training data for rare conditions; use transfer learning from related conditions; implement conservative pricing bounds for low-confidence predictions; document why further reduction is not technically feasible; (2) Step 2 — Implementation of adequate mitigation and control measures (Art.9(5)(b)) — for residual inaccuracy: mandatory human underwriter review for all applications involving rare medical conditions; human override authority for pricing decisions above certain thresholds; enhanced monitoring of claims outcomes for rare condition policyholders; (3) Step 3 — Provision of information and training to deployers (Art.9(5)(c)) — inform the insurance underwriting team of: the AI system's known limitations for rare conditions; the specific conditions and scenarios where accuracy is lower; the mandatory human review process; (4) Acceptability determination — 'acceptable' residual risk is not defined in the Act; the provider must make a documented judgment considering: magnitude of potential harm (inaccurate pricing causing inability to access insurance); likelihood of occurrence; availability of alternatives (human-only underwriting for rare conditions); whether affected persons have meaningful recourse (human review, appeal); (5) Documentation — the residual risk acceptability judgment must be documented in the Art.9 risk management system and referenced in the technical documentation; (6) The AI Review Committee must formally approve the residual risk acceptability determination as part of deployment approval.
notes: Art.9(5) 잔여 리스크 수용 가능성; 단계적 위험 완화

question: How does the EU AI Act's requirement for management accountability (Article 17(1)(j)) translate into board-level reporting requirements for financial institutions?
ground_truth: Article 17(1)(j) requires the Quality Management System to cover the 'responsibilities of management' within the organization. While the EU AI Act does not explicitly mandate board-level AI governance reporting for private financial institutions (unlike financial regulations), the management accountability requirement creates implicit governance obligations: (1) Management responsibility documentation — the QMS must document who in management is responsible for EU AI Act compliance; for a bank, this should identify: the C-suite executive (CRO or CTO) with overall accountability; the AI Review Committee chair with day-to-day governance accountability; business line owners with responsibility for AI systems in their domain; (2) Board reporting obligation inference — effective management accountability requires that boards receive adequate information to exercise oversight; boards that are uninformed about EU AI Act compliance risks cannot exercise meaningful oversight and are not meeting their fiduciary duties under company law; (3) Financial regulatory alignment — banks in the EU are subject to CRD/CRR governance requirements mandating board oversight of material operational risks; EU AI Act compliance failures constitute material operational risks; the board's risk oversight function must encompass AI regulatory compliance; (4) Practical board reporting content: EU AI Act compliance status for all deployed High-Risk AI systems; material AI incidents and serious incident reports; upcoming EU AI Act obligations and implementation progress; regulatory examination findings and remediation; resource adequacy for AI governance; (5) Documentation — board meeting minutes referencing EU AI Act governance discussions provide evidence of management accountability for regulatory purposes; (6) Director liability — in EU jurisdictions, directors who fail to exercise oversight of material regulatory compliance risks may face personal liability under company law.
notes: 경영진 책임; 이사회 보고 체계

question: The bank uses AI to screen job applications and rank candidates for branch manager positions. Annex III §4 covers employment AI. How does the AI Review Committee classify this and what obligations apply?
ground_truth: Annex III §4 explicitly classifies employment and worker management AI as High-Risk. The relevant categories for the bank's recruitment AI: (1) Annex III §4(a) — High-Risk: 'AI systems intended to be used for the recruitment or selection of natural persons, in particular to place targeted job advertisements, to analyse and filter job applications, and to evaluate candidates'; the bank's job application screening and candidate ranking AI directly falls within this definition; (2) Annex III §4(b) — also High-Risk: 'AI systems intended to be used to make decisions affecting terms of work-related relationships, the promotion or termination of work-related contractual relationships, to allocate tasks based on individual behaviour or personal traits, or to monitor and evaluate the performance and behaviour of persons in such relationships'; performance evaluation AI and workforce allocation AI are covered; (3) Full High-Risk obligations apply to recruitment AI: Art.9 risk management system; Art.10 data governance (ensure training data represents diverse candidate pools); Art.11 technical documentation; Art.12 logging of AI decisions and inputs; Art.13 transparency to deployers (HR department); Art.14 human oversight (HR managers must review AI ranking before hiring decisions); Art.15 accuracy and robustness; (4) Specific deployment concern — recruitment AI for branch manager positions involves significant authority over career outcomes; human oversight per Art.14 must be genuine, not perfunctory; (5) FRIA consideration — if the bank is a public entity or provides public services, Art.27 FRIA applies; (6) Worker information (Art.26(7)) — candidates must be informed that AI is used in screening (Art.50(1)) and the HR team using the AI must be informed (Art.26(7)).
legal_basis: EU AI Act Annex III §4(a); Article 6(2)
notes: Annex III §4 채용 AI High-Risk; 명시적 분류

question: After completing conformity assessment for the credit scoring AI, the bank (as provider) must draw up an EU Declaration of Conformity under Article 47. What must this declaration contain?
ground_truth: Article 47 requires providers of High-Risk AI systems to draw up a written EU Declaration of Conformity confirming that the High-Risk AI complies with all applicable requirements. Annex V specifies the content: (1) Provider name and address (Annex V §1); (2) AI system name, description, version, and intended purpose (Annex V §2); (3) Statement that the AI system conforms to all applicable requirements of the EU AI Act and any other Union legislation (Annex V §3); (4) Identification of persons responsible for conformity: where applicable, the name and address of the authorised representative in the EU (if the provider is established outside the EU) (Annex V §4); (5) Information on the conformity assessment procedure followed — whether Annex VI (internal control) or Annex VII (third-party QMS assessment) was used (Annex V §5); (6) If applicable, details of notified body involvement; (7) Reference to harmonized standards or common specifications applied (Annex V §6); (8) Place and date of issue; name, function, and signature of the authorized signatory (Annex V §7); (9) Duration — the declaration must remain valid for the operational lifetime of the AI system or until requirements change; (10) Responsibility — only the provider (not deployers) draws up the EU Declaration of Conformity; the bank as internal developer must sign; if using a vendor's AI, the vendor provides the declaration; (11) Availability — the declaration must be available to competent authorities on request; it is referenced in the EU database registration; (12) The AI Review Committee must verify the Declaration of Conformity is complete and signed by appropriate management authority before EU deployment.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Article 47; Annex V
notes: Art.47 EU 적합성 선언; Annex V 내용

question: The credit scoring AI undergoes a significant model update with new features. Does this trigger a new conformity assessment under the EU AI Act, and how does Article 9 address lifecycle changes?
ground_truth: Significant AI system changes raise compliance implications under the EU AI Act: (1) Article 9(1)-(2) — the risk management system is designed as a continuous iterative process that must be regularly reviewed and updated throughout the AI system's entire lifecycle; model updates must be integrated into the risk management system review cycle; (2) Substantial modification trigger (Art.3(23) definition) — a 'substantial modification' means a change to the High-Risk AI system after its placing on the market or putting into service that affects the AI system's compliance with requirements in Chapter III Section 2, or results in modification of the intended purpose; (3) Consequences of substantial modification — if a model update constitutes a substantial modification: the provider must treat the modified system as a new High-Risk AI system; a new conformity assessment must be completed; a new EU Declaration of Conformity must be drawn up; the EU database registration must be updated; (4) Assessing whether a model update is substantial: adding new features that affect credit decisions — likely substantial; changing the weighting of existing features — likely substantial if it affects accuracy, fairness, or intended purpose; routine recalibration maintaining the same model architecture and performance characteristics — likely not substantial; (5) Documentation requirement (Art.18) — all changes to the High-Risk AI must be documented throughout the system's lifecycle; the technical documentation must be kept up to date; (6) Practical AI Review Committee process — establish a change classification procedure: minor changes — documented in change log, no new conformity assessment; significant changes — full committee review, updated risk management assessment, new conformity assessment if substantial modification threshold met.
notes: 중대한 변경과 신규 적합성 평가; 모델 업데이트 거버넌스

question: Does the EU AI Act apply to the bank's AI systems used solely for internal research and development purposes? What does Article 2 say about the scope of the regulation?
ground_truth: Article 2 establishes the scope of the EU AI Act and includes important exclusions: (1) General scope (Art.2(1)) — the Regulation applies to: providers placing AI systems on the EU market or putting them into service in the EU; operators (deployers) of AI systems located in the EU; providers and deployers of AI systems where the output is used in the EU (regardless of provider's location); (2) Research and development exclusion (Art.2(8)) — AI systems specifically developed and put into service for the sole purpose of scientific research and development are excluded from the Regulation; this provides a genuine safe harbor for pure R&D AI; (3) Limits of the R&D exclusion — 'sole purpose' is strictly interpreted: if the bank's R&D AI is a prototype intended for eventual commercialization, the exclusion may not apply; if the R&D AI is being tested in ways that affect real customers (even in a limited pilot), the exclusion does not apply; pre-commercial pilots and proof-of-concepts testing on actual customer data or actual credit decisions likely fall outside the R&D exclusion; (4) Testing in real conditions — Article 60 provides a specific regime for testing High-Risk AI in real-world conditions ('real world testing') outside AI regulatory sandboxes; this requires specific safeguards rather than simple R&D exclusion; (5) Prototype governance — the AI Review Committee should establish a clear classification for R&D vs. production AI systems; document the intended purpose of all AI development projects; apply EU AI Act governance from the point where an AI system is intended for operational use; (6) National security exclusion (Art.2(3)) — AI for exclusive national security purposes is excluded; not applicable to commercial banking AI.
notes: Art.2 적용 범위; R&D 제외 한계

question: The bank is concerned that complying with EU AI Act transparency requirements might require disclosing proprietary AI model information to competitors or the public. How does Article 78 protect confidential business information?
ground_truth: Article 78 of the EU AI Act establishes confidentiality protections for information disclosed during AI Act compliance and enforcement: (1) Confidentiality obligation — national competent authorities, notified bodies, and other parties involved in AI Act implementation must not disclose information obtained during compliance assessment that constitutes confidential business information, trade secrets, or similar commercially sensitive information; (2) Scope of protection — protected information includes: AI system architecture details; training methodologies; algorithmic parameters; business-sensitive performance data; customer data used in AI systems; (3) Balance with transparency objectives — Article 78 must be interpreted alongside the AI Act's transparency objectives; confidentiality protects against competitor disclosure but does not shield AI systems from regulatory oversight; competent authorities retain access to all information necessary for enforcement; (4) Technical documentation confidentiality — while providers must prepare technical documentation per Art.11, this documentation is not required to be publicly disclosed; it must be available to competent authorities but can be marked as confidential; (5) Public transparency vs. confidentiality — the AI Review Committee can design a two-tier transparency approach: public-facing transparency (Art.13 instructions for use; Art.50 AI disclosures to users; EU database registration information) that does not require disclosure of proprietary model details; regulatory transparency (technical documentation; Art.9 risk management system) available to competent authorities under confidentiality protection; (6) Customer explanation vs. proprietary disclosure — the obligation to explain individual credit decisions to affected persons does not require disclosing the model's full architecture or weights; explanations can describe the major contributing factors without revealing proprietary methods.
notes: Art.78 기밀 보호; 투명성-상업적 비밀 균형

subcategory: Article 14 Oversight Design for HR AI
question: The bank's HR AI for recruitment ranking is a High-Risk system. Article 14(4) specifies oversight capabilities the system must enable. How should the AI Review Committee design human oversight for the HR AI?
ground_truth: Article 14(4) specifies that designated human oversight persons must be able to: (a) fully understand the High-Risk AI system's capabilities and limitations; (b) be aware of automation bias; (c) correctly interpret the High-Risk AI system's output; (d) decide to not use the system or override its output; (e) intervene on or interrupt the system as appropriate. For HR recruitment AI oversight design: (1) Understanding capability (Art.14(4)(a)) — HR managers must receive structured training on: what factors the AI uses to rank candidates (skills, experience, competency test scores); what biases the AI may have (underrepresentation issues in training data); when the AI's rankings are most and least reliable (domain expertise vs. general management positions); (2) Automation bias awareness training (Art.14(4)(b)) — HR managers must be trained to recognize automation bias; studies show that AI-provided rankings can anchor human judgment excessively; training should include: exercises where managers review candidates without AI scores first; case studies of AI ranking errors; awareness of pressure to defer to AI; (3) Output interpretation (Art.14(4)(c)) — HR managers must be able to understand what the numerical ranking or recommendation means and what it does not mean; the AI score is an input to hiring judgment, not a final answer; (4) Override mechanism design (Art.14(4)(d)) — implement a formal override process: any AI ranking must be confirmed or overridden by a named HR manager; overrides must be documented with rationale; aggregate override patterns reviewed quarterly by AI Review Committee; (5) The AI Review Committee should require evidence that HR teams have completed the training program before any High-Risk HR AI is deployed.
notes: HR AI 인간 감독 설계; Art.14(4) 5가지 감독 역량

subcategory: GDPR and EU AI Act Interaction
question: How does the EU AI Act interact with GDPR for the bank's credit scoring AI? Can GDPR obligations satisfy some EU AI Act requirements, or do both sets of obligations apply independently?
ground_truth: The EU AI Act and GDPR operate in parallel and are largely complementary but address different aspects of AI governance. Recital 11 of the EU AI Act acknowledges the relationship with existing EU law. Key interaction points: (1) GDPR automated decision-making (Art.22 GDPR) — GDPR Article 22 provides data subjects the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions that significantly affect them; for credit decisions, data subjects can request human review, access the logic of automated processing, and contest the decision; EU AI Act Art.14 human oversight and Art.13 transparency to deployers complement GDPR Art.22 but are distinct obligations; (2) Data governance overlap — EU AI Act Art.10 data governance requirements (representativeness, bias examination) align with GDPR data accuracy and data minimization principles but go further; GDPR compliance does not automatically satisfy Art.10; (3) Transparency complementarity — GDPR Art.13/14 transparency notices about automated processing complement EU AI Act Art.13/50 transparency obligations; a unified privacy and AI transparency notice can address both; (4) Data subject rights — GDPR rights (access, rectification, erasure, portability) apply to personal data used in credit scoring AI; EU AI Act creates additional transparency rights (Art.86 right to explanation of individual decision-making) that extend beyond GDPR's automated decision-making rights; (5) Accountability alignment — GDPR Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) and EU AI Act Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (Art.27 FRIA) serve similar functions; a coordinated assessment can address both; (6) Special category data — GDPR Art.9 and EU AI Act Art.10(5) both address special category data processing for bias detection with slightly different conditions; the stricter requirement prevails; (7) Practical recommendation — integrate GDPR and EU AI Act compliance through a unified personal data and AI governance framework.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Article 10; Article 86; Recital 11; GDPR Article 22
notes: EU AI Act-GDPR 상호작용; 통합 컴플라이언스 설계

question: Article 86 of the EU AI Act grants individuals the right to explanation of individual decision-making from High-Risk AI. How does this right apply to credit rejection decisions, and what must the bank provide?
ground_truth: Article 86 of the EU AI Act establishes that any person subject to a decision made by a deployer based on the output of a High-Risk AI system listed in Annex III, which produces legal effects or similarly significantly affects them, has the right to obtain from the deployer clear and meaningful explanations of the role the AI system played in the decision-making procedure and the main elements of the decision taken. For credit rejection decisions: (1) Triggering conditions — a credit rejection decision: produces legal effects (denial of a credit contract) or similarly significantly affects the applicant (prevents access to essential financial services); is based on High-Risk AI output (credit scoring AI classified under Annex III §5(b)); meets both conditions — Art.86 applies; (2) Content of explanation — must be: clear — comprehensible to a non-expert; meaningful — substantive, not generic; covers: the role of the AI in the decision-making procedure (was the AI the primary basis? Did human review confirm it?); the main elements of the decision (what factors drove the rejection?); (3) 'Main elements' interpretation — this should include: the primary factors that negatively influenced the credit score; how these factors compare to the institution's requirements; (4) What is NOT required — the full model architecture; exact algorithm weights; complete training data details; (5) Relationship to GDPR Art.22 — Art.86 right to explanation goes beyond GDPR Art.22's automated decision rights, which apply only to 'solely automated' decisions; Art.86 applies even when humans were involved; (6) Practical implementation — the AI Review Committee must ensure: every credit rejection triggers an Art.86 explanation capability; the explanation is generated automatically and available for delivery; the explanation meets the 'clear and meaningful' standard; an appeal process is available.
notes: Art.86 개인 결정 설명권; GDPR Art.22와 구분

question: Article 95 of the EU AI Act encourages voluntary codes of conduct for AI systems that are not High-Risk. How can financial institutions use voluntary codes to demonstrate governance leadership?
ground_truth: Article 95 of the EU AI Act encourages providers and deployers of non-High-Risk AI systems to develop and apply voluntary codes of conduct to voluntarily apply the High-Risk AI requirements (or parts thereof) to their non-High-Risk AI systems. For financial institutions: (1) Scope — applies to AI systems that are not High-Risk under Annex III and not prohibited under Art.5; robo-advisory AI, customer service chatbots (beyond Art.50 obligations), and marketing AI may benefit from voluntary codes; (2) Content of voluntary codes — codes may cover: requirements from Chapter III Section 2 applied voluntarily (risk management, technical documentation, transparency, human oversight); other requirements not in the Act such as environmental sustainability, diversity and inclusion, and digital literacy; (3) AI Office's role (Art.95(3)) — the AI Office may develop common methodologies and tools, issue guidelines, and facilitate exchange of best practices for voluntary codes; financial institutions should engage with AI Office stakeholder processes to shape voluntary code content; (4) Benefits of voluntary code adoption: competitive differentiation — demonstrating higher governance standards than legally required; regulatory goodwill — market surveillance authorities view voluntary code adoption favorably; customer trust — customers who value responsible AI are increasingly discerning; future-proofing — voluntary compliance with High-Risk standards prepares institutions for potential future classification of currently non-High-Risk AI; (5) Financial sector voluntary code — the banking and insurance industry associations could develop a sectoral voluntary code aligned with Art.95; financial institutions should participate in developing such codes through industry associations; (6) Connection to AI기본법 §29(5) — both EU AI Act Art.95 and Korean AI Basic Act §29(5) support voluntary codes; a unified voluntary code satisfying both frameworks is achievable.
notes: Art.95 자율 행동 강령; 비고위험 AI 선제적 거버넌스

question: A Korean bank's credit scoring AI is developed in Korea but the AI's outputs are used to make credit decisions for EU-based customers. Does the EU AI Act apply to this Korean-developed AI system?
ground_truth: Article 2(1)(c) establishes the EU AI Act's extra-territorial reach: the Regulation applies to providers and deployers of AI systems 'where the output produced by the AI system is used in the Union.' For the Korean bank's credit scoring AI: (1) Extra-territorial application confirmed — even though the AI system is developed and operated in Korea, its outputs (credit decisions) are used for EU-based customers in the Union; Article 2(1)(c) applies the Regulation to this Korean-developed AI; (2) Which obligations apply — the bank, as the entity providing and/or deploying AI whose outputs are used in the EU, must comply with all applicable EU AI Act obligations for the credit scoring AI as a High-Risk system under Annex III §5(b); (3) Provider vs. deployer designation — if the Korean bank develops the AI and uses it for its own EU customers, it is simultaneously provider and deployer; both sets of obligations apply; (4) Authorised representative requirement (Art.22) — providers established outside the EU must, by written mandate, designate an authorised representative established in the EU before placing a High-Risk AI system on the EU market; the Korean bank must designate an EU-based authorised representative (a subsidiary, agent, or law firm) for its High-Risk credit scoring AI; (5) EU database registration — registration in the EU database is required, executed through the authorised representative; (6) National competent authority — the bank must identify which EU Member State(s) competent authority has jurisdiction; for a bank operating in multiple EU countries, this may be the authority where the bank's EU operations are headquartered; (7) Practical impact — the AI Review Committee must include EU AI Act compliance as a requirement for ALL AI systems whose outputs affect EU customers, regardless of where the AI is developed.
notes: 역외 적용; 한국 개발 AI의 EU AI Act 적용

subcategory: EU AI Act Financial Governance Summary
question: As a summary: How does the EU AI Act's High-Risk AI framework map to the operational workflow of a financial institution's AI Review Committee for a credit scoring system deployment?
ground_truth: The EU AI Act creates a comprehensive lifecycle governance framework that maps to the AI Review Committee's workflow as follows: (1) Pre-development classification (Article 6 + Annex III) — before development begins, confirm the credit scoring AI falls under Annex III §5(b) High-Risk classification; assess Art.6(3) exceptions (not applicable for credit scoring); check Art.5 prohibited practice exclusions; (2) Design and development phase (Chapter III Section 2) — build compliance into the system: Art.9 risk management system design (continuous, lifecycle-covering); Art.10 data governance (representativeness, bias examination, documentation); Art.15 accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity design; Art.12 automatic logging capability; (3) Pre-deployment conformity assessment (Article 43 + Annex VI or VII) — complete internal control conformity assessment (Annex VI); draw up EU Declaration of Conformity (Art.47); affix CE marking (Art.48); register in EU database (Art.49); prepare technical documentation (Art.11, Annex IV); implement QMS per Art.17; (4) Deployment enablement (Articles 13, 14, 26) — if provider: provide instructions for use to deployer (Art.13); if deployer: implement human oversight (Art.14); assign competent human overseers; inform workers (Art.26(7)); confirm FRIA conducted if required (Art.27); (5) Operational monitoring (Articles 72, 73) — execute post-market monitoring plan (Art.72); report serious incidents within 72 hours (Art.73); cooperate with market surveillance authorities (Art.21); maintain and update technical documentation (Art.18); (6) AI Review Committee role — the committee is the institutional mechanism ensuring all six phases are executed; it approves deployment (after phases 1-3), verifies deployment enablement (phase 4), and oversees ongoing compliance (phase 5); this EU AI Act lifecycle maps to the G1-G4 governance evaluation axes of this research paper's framework.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Chapter III; Articles 6, 9-17, 26, 43, 47-49, 72-73
notes: EU AI Act 통합 요약; AI Review Committee 운영 매핑 — 논문 핵심

question: Article 9(6)-(8) of the EU AI Act mandates testing of High-Risk AI systems. What specific testing requirements must the AI Review Committee implement for the credit scoring AI before and during deployment?
ground_truth: Article 9(6)-(8) establishes mandatory testing requirements for High-Risk AI systems: (1) Purpose of testing (Art.9(6)) — testing must identify the most appropriate and targeted risk management measures and ensure High-Risk AI performs consistently for its intended purpose and complies with Chapter III Section 2 requirements; (2) Real-world condition testing option (Art.9(7)) — testing procedures may include testing in real-world conditions per Article 60; this applies when laboratory testing cannot adequately simulate actual deployment conditions; for credit scoring AI, controlled pilot testing on actual loan applications with heightened human oversight may be appropriate; (3) Pre-market testing obligation (Art.9(8)) — testing must be performed as appropriate throughout the development process AND in any event prior to placing on the market or putting into service; testing cannot be deferred to post-deployment; the 'in any event' language makes pre-deployment testing mandatory without exception; (4) Metrics and thresholds requirement (Art.9(8)) — testing must be carried out against prior defined metrics and probabilistic thresholds appropriate to the intended purpose; for credit scoring AI, the committee must pre-define: AUC/Gini target thresholds; maximum acceptable false rejection rate; fairness metric thresholds (adverse impact ratio minimums); minimum explanation quality scores; (5) Vulnerable population testing (Art.9(9)) — testing must specifically assess impacts on persons under 18 and other vulnerable groups; (6) Testing documentation — all testing procedures, metrics, thresholds, and results must be documented in the technical documentation (Annex IV); (7) AI Review Committee obligation — require submission of pre-defined testing thresholds and test results before granting deployment approval; reject submissions that did not test against pre-defined thresholds.
notes: Art.9 테스트 의무; 사전 정의 임계값 기반 테스트

question: The bank wants to test the credit scoring AI in real-world conditions with actual loan applicants before full deployment. What safeguards does the EU AI Act require under Article 60?
ground_truth: Article 60 provides a framework for testing High-Risk AI in real-world conditions outside AI regulatory sandboxes. For credit scoring AI real-world testing: (1) Provider responsibility (Art.60(1)) — the provider (bank if internally developed) bears responsibility for real-world testing; testing must be conducted in accordance with a testing plan; (2) Testing plan requirements (Art.60(2)) — the plan must cover: the AI system description; testing objectives and conditions; metrics for assessing the AI system; testing duration and location; protection measures for subjects; (3) Fundamental rights safeguards (Art.60(2)(g)) — testing must not lead to 'excessive intrusion into the private sphere' or other fundamental rights violations; for credit testing, this means: applicants must not receive inferior credit terms solely due to their participation in testing; testing cannot disadvantage vulnerable groups; testing must be limited to what is necessary for assessment; (4) Informed consent (Art.61) — persons subject to real-world AI testing must be informed: that an AI system is being tested; the purpose and expected duration; their rights including right to withdraw; that participation is voluntary and refusal has no adverse consequences; for credit applicants in real-world testing: they must consent before being subject to AI-driven credit assessment in the test phase; (5) Data protection — real-world testing data is subject to GDPR; special care regarding sensitive personal financial data; (6) Market surveillance authority notification — providers must notify the relevant national competent authority before commencing real-world testing; (7) AI Review Committee role — approve the testing plan before commencement; verify informed consent procedures are implemented; review testing results before full deployment approval.
notes: Art.60 실세계 조건 테스트; 적절한 안전장치 필요

question: The insurance subsidiary receives a life insurance pricing AI from a vendor (provider). The AI Review Committee examines the vendor's Article 13 instructions for use. What specific performance information must the instructions contain about the AI's accuracy across different population groups?
ground_truth: Article 13(3) specifies detailed content requirements for High-Risk AI instructions for use. Regarding performance across population groups: (1) Article 13(3)(b)(ii) — instructions must include 'the level of accuracy, including its metrics, and the relevant accuracy metrics including the accuracy achieved, as the case may be, in relation to specific persons or groups of persons on which the system is intended to be used'; for life insurance pricing AI this means: overall model accuracy metrics (e.g., loss ratio prediction accuracy, claims prediction AUC); performance disaggregated by: age groups (critically important for life insurance); health status categories; gender where relevant and legally permissible; geographic regions; income brackets; (2) Article 13(3)(b)(iv) — 'the performance of the High-Risk AI system in relation to the persons or groups of persons on which the system is intended to be used'; this goes further than just accuracy — it requires explanation of how the AI performs differently for different groups; (3) Article 13(3)(b)(iii) — 'known or foreseeable circumstances, related to the use of the High-Risk AI system in accordance with its intended purpose or under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse, which may lead to risks to the health and safety or fundamental rights referred to in Article 9(2)'; vendor must disclose known population groups for whom the model is less reliable or potentially biased; (4) Practical implication for AI Review Committee — reject vendor-provided instructions that provide only aggregate performance metrics without demographic disaggregation; require vendors to demonstrate testing across all relevant demographic segments; (5) Vendor contract clause — include a contractual requirement for vendors to update Art.13 instructions whenever performance data reveals new demographic disparities.
notes: Art.13(3) 집단별 성능 정보; 보험 AI 인구통계 성능 공개

question: The bank (as deployer) discovers that its vendor-provided credit scoring AI is producing significantly discriminatory outcomes for a specific ethnic minority group in France. What notification obligations arise under Article 26(5), and what is the timeline?
ground_truth: Article 26(5) creates an immediate notification obligation when deployers identify previously unknown risks: 'Where the deployer identifies a risk within the meaning of Article 9(2) and that risk was not previously identified by the provider, it shall, without undue delay, inform the provider of the AI system as well as the relevant market surveillance authority.' For discriminatory credit scoring outcomes: (1) Triggering condition — the discrimination constitutes a risk to fundamental rights (equal treatment, non-discrimination) within the meaning of Art.9(2)(a); this was presumably not previously identified by the vendor-provider (otherwise it should have been in the Art.13 instructions for use); Art.26(5) notification is therefore triggered; (2) Immediate action sequence: Step 1 — notify the vendor-provider 'without undue delay' (no specific timeframe; immediately upon confirmation of the discriminatory pattern); Step 2 — notify the relevant market surveillance authority in France 'without undue delay'; Step 3 — suspend use of the AI for affected customer segment pending investigation (Art.26(4) monitoring and suspension obligation); (3) What to report to the authority: identity of provider and deployer; description of the AI system and its function; nature of the identified risk (discriminatory outcomes for specific ethnic group); scope (number of affected persons, time period, geographic area); immediate actions taken (suspension, notification to provider); (4) Connection to Art.73 serious incident reporting — if the discrimination constitutes a serious incident (violation of EU law protecting fundamental rights at scale), Art.73 serious incident reporting may also apply; (5) Regulatory consequence — market surveillance authorities will investigate; the vendor-provider faces potential enforcement action; the bank may also face regulatory scrutiny for its deployment monitoring practices.
notes: Art.26(5) 리스크 통보; 차별 발견 시 즉각 조치

question: Article 9(9) of the EU AI Act requires consideration of adverse impacts on persons under 18 and other vulnerable groups. How should the AI Review Committee implement this requirement for the credit scoring AI?
ground_truth: Article 9(9) requires that when implementing the risk management system, providers give consideration to whether the High-Risk AI system is likely to have an adverse impact on persons under the age of 18 and, as appropriate, other vulnerable groups. For credit scoring AI: (1) Persons under 18 — credit scoring AI used for lending to minors is uncommon (most lending requires legal capacity); however: if the AI processes guarantor information or family financial data that may include minors; if the AI is used for student loan products with minor co-signers; the committee should assess these edge cases; (2) Vulnerable groups beyond minors — 'other vulnerable groups' is not exhaustively defined but clearly includes: elderly persons with potential cognitive decline or digital literacy limitations; persons with disabilities (physical or cognitive) that may affect their financial data patterns; financially distressed persons who may be particularly harmed by incorrect AI credit decisions; persons with limited financial literacy; persons from communities historically underserved by formal financial systems; (3) Assessment methodology — for each vulnerable group: identify how the AI system may disproportionately affect them; assess whether training data adequately represents them; test model performance specifically for these groups; evaluate whether error rates are disproportionately higher; (4) Mitigation measures for vulnerable groups: mandatory human review for credit decisions affecting identified vulnerable groups; lower AI-driven rejection thresholds triggering automatic human escalation; enhanced explanation quality standards for adverse decisions affecting vulnerable groups; (5) Documentation — Art.9(9) considerations must be documented in the Art.9 risk management system and referenced in technical documentation; (6) AI Review Committee gate — require explicit vulnerable group impact analysis as a pre-deployment submission requirement.
notes: Art.9(9) 취약계층 영향 평가; 미성년자 및 기타 취약 그룹

question: Article 18 of the EU AI Act requires providers to keep technical documentation for a defined period after the AI system is placed on the market. What are the retention requirements and how should the AI Review Committee manage document lifecycle?
ground_truth: Article 18 establishes documentation retention obligations for High-Risk AI providers: (1) Core retention obligation (Art.18(1)) — providers must keep technical documentation, logs (where within their control), and the EU Declaration of Conformity for a period of 10 years after the High-Risk AI system has been placed on the EU market or put into service; (2) Serious incident documentation — documentation related to serious incidents reported under Art.73 must be retained and available for authority review; (3) Relevance for the bank: as provider (internally developed AI) — the 10-year retention clock starts when the AI system is first deployed in the EU; as deployer (vendor AI) — the vendor-provider has the 10-year retention obligation; the bank should contractually require vendors to maintain documentation for the required period and confirm continued access; (4) What must be retained: complete technical documentation per Annex IV (at all versions); EU Declaration of Conformity; logs of AI system operation to the extent within the provider's control; records of the quality management system (Art.17); records of conformity assessment procedures; any serious incident reports; (5) Version control — technical documentation must be kept up to date (Art.11(1)); historical versions must also be retained to enable reconstruction of past AI system states for legal and regulatory purposes; (6) Post-decommissioning obligations — the 10-year retention period runs from market placement, not decommissioning; documentation obligations survive AI system retirement; (7) AI Review Committee obligation — establish a document lifecycle management system ensuring 10-year retention for all High-Risk AI technical documentation from first EU deployment date.
notes: Art.18 문서 보존 10년; 퇴역 후에도 지속

question: Article 21 of the EU AI Act requires High-Risk AI providers to cooperate with national competent authorities on request. What does this obligation entail for a Korean bank operating in the EU, and how should the AI Review Committee prepare?
ground_truth: Article 21 requires providers of High-Risk AI to cooperate with national competent authorities in performing their tasks. For a Korean bank with EU operations: (1) Scope of cooperation obligation (Art.21(1)) — upon a reasoned request, providers must take all necessary steps to: provide competent authorities with access to technical documentation and information necessary to assess AI system compliance; enable market surveillance authorities to inspect AI systems; provide information about the AI system's capabilities and limitations; (2) Cooperation with test access — upon request, providers must allow market surveillance authorities access to the AI system itself for assessment purposes; this may include providing access to model APIs or controlled test environments; (3) National contact point — providers established outside the EU must designate an authorised representative (Art.22) who serves as the point of contact for competent authority cooperation; the Korean bank's EU authorised representative handles cooperation requests; (4) Timeliness — cooperation must be prompt; 'necessary steps' implies active engagement rather than passive response; (5) Confidentiality protection — information provided under Art.21 is subject to Art.78 confidentiality obligations; competent authorities cannot disclose commercially sensitive information to third parties; (6) Preparation for the AI Review Committee: maintain examination-ready documentation packages for all High-Risk AI systems; designate a named regulatory liaison for EU AI Act authority interactions; establish a protocol for responding to authority requests (triage → legal review → response within target timeline); train staff on cooperation obligations; (7) Financial regulator relationship — in many EU jurisdictions, the financial services regulator (e.g., BaFin, ACPR) may be designated as the competent authority for financial AI; leverage existing regulatory relationships.
notes: Art.21 당국 협력 의무; 한국 은행의 EU 당국 대응

question: Article 62 of the EU AI Act provides support measures for SMEs and startups. Can smaller financial institutions (e.g., credit unions, small fintech lenders) benefit from these measures?
ground_truth: Article 62 establishes measures to support SMEs including startups in EU Member States: (1) Priority access to regulatory sandboxes (Art.62(1)(a)) — SMEs and startups are given priority access to AI regulatory sandboxes established under Art.57; smaller financial institutions can use sandboxes to test novel credit AI or insurance AI under regulatory supervision before full compliance burden applies; (2) Separate communication channels (Art.62(1)(b)) — competent authorities must establish specific communication channels for SMEs and startups to raise questions and receive information on implementation; smaller financial institutions can use these channels to seek clarity on how EU AI Act applies to their specific AI systems; (3) Dedicated support (Art.62(1)(c)) — Member States may provide tailored guidance, simplified compliance documents, and templates specifically designed for SMEs; smaller financial institutions should actively seek these resources; (4) Reduced fees for SMEs (Art.62(2)) — for conformity assessment procedures (Art.43), Member States may establish reduced fees for microenterprises, small and medium-sized enterprises; (5) Definition of SME — under EU definition, SMEs are enterprises with fewer than 250 employees and either annual turnover ≤€50M or balance sheet ≤€43M; most credit unions and smaller fintech lenders qualify; (6) Practical guidance for smaller financial institutions: identify the EU Member State(s) competent authority offering SME support; request access to regulatory sandboxes for novel AI testing; use SME-simplified compliance templates where available; join industry associations providing collective compliance guidance; (7) Equal compliance obligations — SME support reduces compliance burden but does not reduce the substantive compliance obligations; High-Risk AI must still meet all Chapter III requirements.
notes: Art.62 중소기업 지원; 소규모 금융기관 EU AI Act 대응

question: Article 111 of the EU AI Act addresses AI systems already placed on the market before the Act's application. The bank has a credit scoring AI deployed in the EU before August 2026. What transitional provisions apply?
ground_truth: Article 111 establishes transitional provisions for AI systems already on the market before the EU AI Act's full application. For credit scoring AI deployed before August 2026: (1) General transitional rule (Art.111(1)) — High-Risk AI systems already placed on the EU market or put into service before the application date (August 2026) are exempt from Chapter III obligations IF they do not undergo a substantial modification after that date; this is a grandfather clause for pre-existing AI systems; (2) 'Substantial modification' significance — if the pre-existing credit scoring AI undergoes a substantial modification after August 2026: the transitional exemption is lost; the modified system must comply with all Chapter III obligations; this creates a strategic AI governance consideration: minor updates preserve the exemption; major redesigns or architectural changes trigger full compliance; (3) Voluntary compliance option — providers of pre-existing systems may voluntarily comply with all EU AI Act requirements before they are legally required; this is advisable as: regulatory examination of pre-existing systems is still possible; voluntary compliance demonstrates good faith; prepares the institution for the eventual compliance obligation when substantial modification occurs; (4) Time-limited exemption — the transitional exemption does not last indefinitely; the Commission reviews transitional provisions; as substantial modifications occur over time, compliance becomes progressively required; (5) AI Review Committee action plan for pre-existing AI: catalog all High-Risk AI systems deployed before August 2026; document their deployment dates to establish baseline; classify planned changes as substantial modification (full compliance required) or non-substantial (exemption preserved); develop a compliance roadmap for pre-existing systems as they undergo updates.
notes: Art.111 경과 조치; 기존 배포 AI 시스템 유예

question: Article 40 of the EU AI Act references harmonised standards for AI. What is the significance of harmonised standards for financial institutions' AI compliance, and what presumption does Article 42 create?
ground_truth: Articles 40 and 42 establish the role of harmonised standards in EU AI Act compliance: (1) Article 40 — Harmonised Standards: the European Commission can request standardisation bodies (CEN, CENELEC, ETSI) to develop harmonised standards for the EU AI Act; these standards will specify technical requirements for compliance with specific EU AI Act provisions; for financial AI, standards may address: AI risk management systems (aligned with ISO/IEC 23894); AI trustworthiness characteristics (aligned with ISO/IEC TR 24028); testing methodologies for credit scoring AI; fairness metrics and measurement methodologies; (2) Article 42 — Presumption of Conformity: 'High-risk AI systems that are in conformity with harmonised standards or parts thereof the references of which have been published in the Official Journal of the European Union shall be presumed to be in conformity with the requirements set out in Chapter III Section 2 of this Regulation'; this creates a legal safe harbor — compliance with harmonised standards presumptively satisfies EU AI Act requirements; (3) Practical benefit for financial institutions: standardised test methodologies provide clarity on how to demonstrate Art.9 testing compliance; standardised documentation formats simplify Art.11 technical documentation preparation; presumption of conformity reduces regulatory uncertainty and litigation risk; (4) Current standards landscape (as of 2026): ISO/IEC 42001 (AI Management Systems) is a candidate for harmonised standard status; ISO/IEC 23894 (AI Risk Management) aligns with Art.9; work is ongoing by CEN/CENELEC JTC 21; (5) AI Review Committee action — adopt available ISO/IEC AI standards now; monitor Official Journal for harmonised standard publications; update governance framework when standards are published to benefit from presumption of conformity.
notes: Art.40/42 조화 표준; 적합 추정 법적 효과

question: The AI Review Committee is designing a standardized technical documentation template for all High-Risk AI systems. What must Annex IV documentation include for the credit scoring AI specifically?
ground_truth: Annex IV specifies the minimum content for technical documentation of High-Risk AI systems. For credit scoring AI, the standardized template must include: (1) General description (Annex IV §1): intended purpose (creditworthiness evaluation for personal loans up to X amount); version information; how it interacts with other hardware/software; how the AI system is made available (licensed software, API, embedded in loan origination system); third-party tools and components used; (2) Detailed description of elements and development process (Annex IV §2): the logic of the AI: the overall model architecture (gradient boosting, neural network, etc.); training methodology including data preparation, feature engineering, model selection; training datasets with description of source, characteristics, coverage (geographic, demographic, temporal), and quality assurance measures; (3) Validation and testing (Annex IV §3): results of accuracy and robustness measurements; test sets used (description and characteristics); test methodology; performance metrics including disaggregated performance by demographic group; testing against pre-defined thresholds per Art.9(8); fairness testing results; (4) Monitoring and control (Annex IV §4): post-market monitoring plan per Art.72; logging capabilities per Art.12; (5) Risk management (Annex IV §5): summary of the Art.9 risk management system; identified risks and mitigation measures; residual risk assessment; (6) Changes made to the system throughout its lifecycle (Annex IV §6): change log with description of changes and assessment of whether they constitute substantial modifications; (7) Standards applied (Annex IV §7): list of harmonized standards applied or reference to common specifications; (8) EU Declaration of Conformity (Annex IV §8): copy of the Art.47 declaration; (9) Post-market monitoring (Annex IV §9): description of the Art.72 system.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Article 11; Annex IV
notes: Annex IV 기술 문서 표준 템플릿; 신용 AI 특화

question: The bank uses generative AI to create personalized investment summaries and financial health reports for customers. Article 50(2) requires marking of AI-generated content. How must this marking be implemented?
ground_truth: Article 50(2) requires providers of AI systems that generate synthetic audio, image, video, or text content to ensure the outputs are marked in a machine-readable format and detectable as artificially generated.
