Preparation for potential investment AI High-Risk classification: (1) Monitoring Commission proposals — Article 7 delegated acts require: Commission impact assessment; European Parliament scrutiny period; Member State consultation; AI Board input; Financial sector associations will monitor and respond to proposals; (2) Advance compliance preparation — even before formal classification, voluntarily implement High-Risk AI standards for robo-advisory AI: begin developing Art.9 risk management documentation; assess training data representativeness per Art.10; design explanation capability meeting Art.86 standards; plan human oversight structure per Art.14; (3) Time advantage — advance preparation means: shorter compliance timeline when classification becomes mandatory; demonstration of good governance to regulators; reduced implementation cost (building in from design vs. retrofit); (4) Risk classification criteria — Art.7 requires demonstration that the AI 'poses a risk of harm to the health and safety of persons or adverse impact on fundamental rights that is equivalent to or greater than the risk of harm posed by the high-risk AI systems already referred to in Annex III'; for investment AI: adverse investment outcomes (financial harm) and lack of explainability are potential grounds; (5) Voluntary Code of Conduct (Art.95) — apply High-Risk standards voluntarily through Art.95 codes of conduct; this demonstrates regulatory readiness; (6) Sandbox testing (Art.57) — use EU regulatory sandboxes to develop and test High-Risk AI compliance approaches for robo-advisory AI before formal classification; (7) Industry input — engage with ESMA and EBA who may provide input to the Commission on investment AI risk assessment.
notes: Art.7 Annex III 확장; 투자 AI 선제적 준비

subcategory: EU AI Act and Financial Inclusion
question: The EU AI Act's recitals reference the EU's commitment to fundamental rights and equality. How should the AI Review Committee use EU AI Act obligations (particularly Arts. 9, 10, and 14) to proactively advance financial inclusion, not just avoid harm?
ground_truth: The EU AI Act is fundamentally a protective regulation, but its requirements also create opportunities to advance financial inclusion: (1) Article 10(2) representativeness as inclusion tool — the requirement that training data be 'sufficiently representative' creates affirmative obligations to ensure underserved populations are in the training data; the AI Review Committee can interpret this proactively: require training data to include sufficient representation of thin-file customers (limited credit history), first-generation immigrants, self-employed individuals, and rural populations who are systematically underrepresented in traditional credit data; (2) Article 10(2)(f) bias examination as inclusion tool — the bias examination requirement creates an opportunity to identify and correct biases that exclude creditworthy individuals from underserved communities; framing bias correction as advancing credit access, not just avoiding discrimination; (3) Article 9(9) vulnerable groups as inclusion lens — the requirement to assess adverse impacts on vulnerable groups should include assessment of whether the AI is unnecessarily excluding vulnerable but creditworthy populations; (4) Article 14 human oversight as inclusion safety valve — configure mandatory human review not only for AI-flagged risks but also for cases where AI may be uncertain due to non-traditional financial profiles; human oversight can catch creditworthy individuals that AI underserves due to training data limitations; (5) Alternative data opportunity — EU AI Act Art.10(5)'s permission to process special category data for bias detection and correction creates a framework for incorporating alternative creditworthiness data (utility payments, rental payments) to expand credit access; (6) The AI Review Committee should include financial inclusion metrics in its AI performance evaluation framework alongside traditional accuracy and fairness metrics.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Article 10(2); Article 9(9); Article 14
notes: EU AI Act와 금융 포용; 규제 의무를 포용 기회로 전환

question: The AI Review Committee manages a credit scoring AI subject to all three regulations: Korean AI Basic Act, EU AI Act, and NIST AI RMF. Compare the three frameworks' approaches to the most critical governance dimension — risk identification and management — and identify where they converge and diverge.
ground_truth: Comparative analysis of risk identification and management across the three frameworks for credit scoring AI: (1) NIST AI RMF — MAP and MEASURE functions: Approach: voluntary, flexible, process-oriented; identifies risks through a structured four-function cycle (Govern, Map, Measure, Manage); MAP function establishes context and identifies risks through stakeholder engagement, system categorization, and impact characterization; MEASURE function quantifies and tracks risks using quantitative and qualitative methods; Strength: comprehensive, sector-agnostic, flexible to institution size; Limitation: voluntary and non-prescriptive; does not specify required thresholds or metrics; (2) Korean AI기본법 — Article 34(1)(1) and Article 32: Approach: mandatory, principle-based for 고영향 AI; requires establishing and operating a risk management plan (위험관리방안) covering risk identification, assessment, and mitigation; Article 32 adds lifecycle risk management and safety incident monitoring for large-compute AI; Strength: clear legal obligation with enforcement (Article 40 investigation, Article 43 penalties); Limitation: implementation details delegated to Presidential Decree (still pending); less prescriptive than EU AI Act; (3) EU AI Act — Article 9: Approach: mandatory, most prescriptive of the three; specifies four required steps: (a) identification and analysis of known and foreseeable risks; (b) estimation and evaluation during use AND foreseeable misuse; (c) evaluation based on post-market monitoring data; (d) targeted risk management measures; requires testing against pre-defined thresholds; requires assessment for vulnerable populations; Strength: most detailed and legally clear; provides integration with existing financial risk frameworks (Art.9(10)); Limitation: compliance burden; prescriptiveness may reduce flexibility; (4) Convergence points: All three require: continuous lifecycle risk management; identification of risks to human rights/fundamental rights; documentation of risks and mitigations; human oversight as a risk mitigation; post-deployment monitoring; (5) Divergence points: Prescriptiveness: EU AI Act > AI기본법 > NIST AI RMF; Mandatory nature: EU AI Act = AI기본법 > NIST AI RMF (voluntary); Specificity: EU AI Act specifies four steps; AI기본법 identifies general principles with delegated detail; NIST provides guidance not requirements; Sector integration: EU AI Act Art.9(10) explicitly integrates with financial regulation; AI기본법 §34(3) provides equivalence; NIST is inherently flexible; (6) Integration recommendation for AI Review Committee: use NIST AI RMF MAP/MEASURE as the flexible analytical framework for risk identification; structure the risk management system to satisfy EU AI Act Art.9's four mandatory steps; document the integrated system to satisfy AI기본법 Art.34(1)(1); this achieves simultaneous compliance with all three frameworks.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Article 9; AI기본법 제34조; NIST AI RMF MAP/MEASURE
notes: 3개 규제 리스크 관리 비교; 논문 3장 통합 프레임워크 핵심

subcategory: EU AI Act Complete Governance Summary
question: As a comprehensive summary integrating all three regulations: A Korean bank deploys a credit scoring AI for EU customers. Map the complete EU AI Act governance lifecycle alongside the Korean AI Basic Act and NIST AI RMF equivalents to create an integrated governance framework for the AI Review Committee.
ground_truth: The integrated three-regulation governance lifecycle for a Korean bank's EU credit scoring AI: PHASE 1 — Classification and Scope: EU AI Act: Annex III §5(b) High-Risk; Art.2(1)(c) extra-territorial application; Art.22 authorised representative required; Korean AI기본법: §2(4)(사) 고영향 AI (대출 심사); §33 pre-deployment 고영향 AI review; §4 extra-territorial if affecting Korean users; NIST AI RMF: MAP 1.1 context establishment; MAP 1.5 risk tolerance documentation; PHASE 2 — Design and Development: EU AI Act: Art.9 risk management system (four steps); Art.10 data governance (representativeness, bias examination); Art.15 accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity design; Art.12 logging capability; Korean AI기본법: §34(1)(1) 위험관리방안 수립; §34(1)(2) 설명 방안 (explainability built-in); §34(1)(4) 인간 감독 체계 설계; NIST AI RMF: MAP 2.1 system categorization; MEASURE 2.5 pre-deployment validation; MEASURE 2.11 fairness testing; PHASE 3 — Pre-Deployment Conformity: EU AI Act: Art.43/Annex VI conformity assessment (self-assessment for §5(b)); Art.47 EU Declaration of Conformity; Art.48 CE marking; Art.49 EU database registration; Art.17 QMS implementation; Korean AI기본법: §30 검·인증 (voluntary effort); §34(1)(5) 문서화 패키지 완성; §35 영향평가 실시; NIST AI RMF: MEASURE 2.3 performance against thresholds; MAP 5.1 impact characterization; MANAGE 1.1 go/no-go decision; PHASE 4 — Deployment: EU AI Act: Art.13 instructions for use to deployer; Art.14 human oversight implementation; Art.26(7) worker information; Art.27 FRIA (if applicable); Art.50 customer AI disclosure; Korean AI기본법: §31(1) 사전 고지 (AI 사용 통보); §34(1)(3) 이용자 보호 방안 시행; §34(1)(4) 인간 감독 운영; NIST AI RMF: GOVERN 2.1 roles/responsibilities; MANAGE 1.3 risk response; GOVERN 4.3 information sharing; PHASE 5 — Ongoing Operation: EU AI Act: Art.72 post-market monitoring; Art.73 serious incident reporting (72hr); Art.26(4)-(5) monitoring and suspension; Art.86 explanation right on request; Art.18 10-year documentation retention; Korean AI기본법: §32(1)(2) 안전사고 모니터링; §34(1)(1) 위험관리방안 지속 운영; §40 당국 조사 대응; NIST AI RMF: MEASURE 2.4 production monitoring; MANAGE 4.1 incident response; MANAGE 4.2 continuous improvement; This integrated lifecycle framework is the core design artifact of this research paper's AI Review Committee support chatbot.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Chapter III; AI기본법 제2조·제31조~제35조; NIST AI RMF Govern-Map-Measure-Manage
notes: 3개 규제 통합 거버넌스 생애주기; 논문 3·5장 핵심 통합 프레임워크 — 연구의 최종 기여
